title: Repo and Reverse Repo Agreements - Federal Reserve Bank of New York

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: The New York Fed's Open Market Trading Desk conducts repo and reverse repo transactions as directed by the FOMC to implement monetary policy and maintain the federal funds rate within its target range. Repos temporarily increase reserve balances while reverse repos temporarily reduce them. The Desk conducts both overnight reverse repo (ON RRP) and standing repo (SRP) operations each business day at FOMC-set rates.
key_points: Repo: Desk purchases securities from counterparty with agreement to resell, temporarily increasing reserve balances (economically similar to a collateralized loan)
Reverse repo: Desk sells securities to counterparty with agreement to repurchase, temporarily reducing reserve balances (economically similar to collateralized borrowing)
ON RRP provides a floor under overnight interest rates by offering eligible institutions an alternative risk-free investment when rates fall below IORB
Standing repo operations provide a ceiling on money market rates by limiting upward pressure
Eligible collateral includes U.S. Treasury securities, agency debt, and agency mortgage-backed securities
Counterparties include primary dealers, banks, money market mutual funds, and government sponsored enterprises
why_it_matters: These operations form the technical backbone of the federal funds rate target range. By conducting daily repo and reverse repo operations, the Fed actively manages liquidity in the banking system to keep short-term rates stable. ON RRP and standing repo act as guardrails, preventing rates from drifting outside the FOMC's target range.
macro_context: Repo and reverse repo operations are part of the Federal Reserve's standard monetary policy toolkit, conducted daily to maintain the fed funds rate within the FOMC's target range. ON RRP and standing repo serve as the lower and upper bounds of the interest rate corridor, respectively.
market_structure_context: The ON RRP and standing repo facilities form the boundaries of the Fed's interest rate corridor system, directly shaping repo market dynamics, short-term funding costs, and money market fund flows. This structure is fundamental to understanding how the Fed implements monetary policy through money market operations.
